sections.title: Poem Summary
sections.content: 'The Listeners' is a mysterious poem by Walter de la Mare. It describes a lonely Traveller who arrives on horseback at a lone house in the middle of a forest on a moonlit night. He knocks on the door repeatedly and calls out, 'Is there anybody there?' However, he receives no answer from any living person. Inside the house, there is a 'host of phantom listeners' (ghosts or spirits) who hear his voice but remain silent. The Traveller is left perplexed and confused by the eerie silence. The poem perfectly captures a supernatural, eerie, and suspenseful atmosphere, contrasting the loud calls of the living Traveller with the absolute stillness of the phantom listeners.

sections.title: About the Author
sections.content: Walter de la Mare (1873–1956) was an English poet, short story writer, and novelist. He is best known for his magical and mysterious works written for children. 'The Listeners' is widely considered to be his most famous poem.

sections.title: Key Elements and Atmosphere
sections.content:
• The poem creates a strong sense of mystery through its setting and word choices.
• - Natural Elements: Moonlit door, forest's ferny floor, bird flying from a turret, leaf-fringed sill.
• - Mysterious Elements: Phantom listeners, lone house, dark stair, empty hall, voice from the world of men.
• - The Traveller represents the living world, while the silent house represents the supernatural or the unknown.

sections.title: Glossary
sections.content:
• champed - bit and chewed upon noisily
• ferny - consisting of flowerless plants (ferns)
• turret - a small tower that projects from the wall of a building
• smote - knocked loudly
• sill - a horizontal piece of timber beneath a window or a door
• perplexed - confused
• thronging - crowding
• hearkening - listening attentively

sections.title: Book Back Exercises: True or False
sections.content:
• The Traveller came at noon. - False (Quote: 'Knocking on the moonlit door')
• The house was situated in the forest. - True (Quote: 'Of the forest's ferny floor')
• There were ghostly listeners inside the house. - True (Quote: 'But only a host of phantom listeners')
• The Traveller came to the house by bicycle. - False (Quote: 'And his horse in the silence champed the grasses')
• The phantoms answered and asked the traveller to go away. - False (Quote: 'Stood listening in the quiet of the moonlight')

sections.title: Book Back Exercises: Q & A
sections.content:
• Who is the speaker? - The speaker is an unseen narrator or observer describing the event.
• What was the horse doing? - The horse was champing (chewing noisily) the grasses on the forest's ferny floor.
• Who were the listeners? - The listeners were a host of phantoms (ghosts) dwelling in the lone house.
• How did the Traveller feel when nobody answered? - He felt perplexed (confused) and stood still.
• What kind of feeling does the poem create? - The poem creates a feeling of mystery, suspense, and eerie supernatural presence.

sections.title: Poetic Devices
sections.content:
• Alliteration: 'forest’s ferny floor' (repetition of the 'f' sound).
▸ 2. Rhyming Words:
• - head / said
• - sill / still
• - then / men
• - hall / call
• Words used to create a sense of mystery: phantom, lone house, dark stair, empty hall, hearkening.

questions.question: Who is the main human character in the poem 'The Listeners'?
questions.explanation: The poem revolves around 'the Traveller' who knocks on the moonlit door.

questions.question: What time of the day does the poem take place?
questions.explanation: The poem takes place at night, as indicated by the 'moonlit door' and 'faint moonbeams'.

questions.question: What were the exact words the Traveller said when he knocked on the door?
questions.explanation: The poem begins with the Traveller asking, 'Is there anybody there?'

questions.question: What animal accompanied the Traveller?
questions.explanation: The text mentions 'And his horse in the silence champed the grasses'.

questions.question: What was the horse doing in the silence of the forest?
questions.explanation: The horse was 'champing' (biting and chewing noisily) the grasses.

questions.question: What is the meaning of the word 'champed'?
questions.explanation: According to the glossary, 'champed' means to bite and chew upon noisily.

questions.question: How is the floor of the forest described in the poem?
questions.explanation: The poem describes it as 'the forest's ferny floor'.

questions.question: What does the word 'ferny' refer to?
questions.explanation: Ferny means consisting of ferns, which are a type of flowerless plant.

questions.question: What flew up over the Traveller's head?
questions.explanation: The poem states, 'And a bird flew up out of the turret, Above the Traveller’s head'.

questions.question: Where did the bird fly out from?
questions.explanation: The bird flew up 'out of the turret'.

questions.question: What is a 'turret'?
options: A small tower that projects from the wall of a building
questions.explanation: A turret is a small tower that projects vertically from the wall of a building.

questions.question: What did the Traveller do after the bird flew up?
options: He smote upon the door again a second time
questions.explanation: After the bird flew up, 'he smote upon the door again a second time'.

questions.question: What does the word 'smote' mean?
questions.explanation: According to the glossary, 'smote' means knocked loudly.

questions.question: Who descended to the Traveller after he knocked?
questions.explanation: The poem explicitly states, 'But no one descended to the Traveller'.

questions.question: How is the window sill described in the poem?
questions.explanation: The poem mentions 'No head from the leaf-fringed sill'.

questions.question: What is a 'sill'?
options: A horizontal piece of timber beneath a window or a door
questions.explanation: A sill is the horizontal piece of timber or stone at the bottom of a window or door.

questions.question: What was the colour of the Traveller's eyes?
questions.explanation: The poem describes that no one looked 'into his grey eyes'.

questions.question: How did the Traveller stand when nobody answered him?
questions.explanation: He stood 'perplexed and still' because there was no response.

questions.question: What does the word 'perplexed' mean?
questions.explanation: Perplexed means feeling completely baffled or confused.

questions.question: Who was actually inside the lone house?
questions.explanation: The house was occupied by 'a host of phantom listeners'.

questions.question: What were the phantom listeners doing?
options: Stood listening in the quiet of the moonlight
questions.explanation: The phantoms 'Stood listening in the quiet of the moonlight'.

questions.question: Whose voice were the phantoms listening to?
options: The voice from the world of men
questions.explanation: They were listening to the Traveller's call, described as 'that voice from the world of men'.

questions.question: Where were the phantom listeners thronging?
options: On the faint moonbeams on the dark stair
questions.explanation: They 'Stood thronging the faint moonbeams on the dark stair'.

questions.question: What does the word 'thronging' mean?
questions.explanation: Thronging means crowding or gathering tightly together in a large number.

questions.question: Where does the dark stair go down to?
questions.explanation: The dark stair 'goes down to the empty hall'.

questions.question: What were the phantoms doing in the stirred and shaken air?
questions.explanation: They were 'Hearkening in an air stirred and shaken'.

questions.question: What is the meaning of 'hearkening'?
questions.explanation: Hearkening is an old-fashioned word meaning to listen attentively.

questions.question: What caused the air to be 'stirred and shaken'?
options: The flapping wings of the bird
questions.explanation: The air was stirred and shaken 'By the lonely Traveller's call'.

questions.question: Who is the author of the poem 'The Listeners'?
questions.explanation: The poem was written by the English poet Walter de la Mare.

questions.question: What is the lifespan of Walter de la Mare?
questions.explanation: Walter de la Mare lived from 1873 to 1956.

questions.question: Walter de la Mare is best known for his works written for _________.
questions.explanation: He is an English poet and novelist best known for his works for children.

questions.question: Which of the following is considered Walter de la Mare's most famous poem?
questions.explanation: 'The Listeners' is said to be his most famous poem.

questions.question: True or False Book Back Check: The Traveller came at noon.
questions.explanation: It is False. The quote 'Knocking on the moonlit door' indicates he came at night.

questions.question: True or False Book Back Check: The house was situated in the forest.
questions.explanation: It is True. The poem mentions 'Of the forest's ferny floor'.

questions.question: True or False Book Back Check: There were ghostly listeners inside the house.
questions.explanation: It is True. The poem talks about 'a host of phantom listeners'.

questions.question: True or False Book Back Check: The Traveller came to the house by bicycle.
questions.explanation: It is False. The Traveller came on horseback ('And his horse in the silence').

questions.question: True or False Book Back Check: The phantoms answered and asked the traveller to go away.
questions.explanation: It is False. They did not answer; they just 'Stood listening in the quiet'.

questions.question: Which figure of speech is used in the phrase 'forest’s ferny floor'?
questions.explanation: The repetition of the 'f' consonant sound in 'forest', 'ferny', and 'floor' is Alliteration.

questions.question: Pick out the rhyming word for 'head' from the poem.
questions.explanation: In the poem, 'head' rhymes with 'said'.

questions.question: Pick out the rhyming word for 'sill' from the poem.
questions.explanation: In the poem, 'sill' rhymes with 'still'.

questions.question: Pick out the rhyming word for 'then' from the poem.
questions.explanation: In the poem, 'then' rhymes with 'men'.

questions.question: Pick out the rhyming word for 'hall' from the poem.
questions.explanation: In the poem, 'hall' rhymes with 'call'.

questions.question: Which of the following phrases from the poem creates a sense of mystery?
questions.explanation: The phrase 'phantom listeners' introduces supernatural elements, creating a deep sense of mystery.

questions.question: Who is the speaker in the poem?
questions.explanation: The poem is told in the third person by an unseen narrator describing the Traveller's actions.

questions.question: What overall feeling or mood does the poem create?
questions.explanation: The silent phantoms, the lone house in the forest, and the unanswered calls create an eerie, mysterious feeling.

questions.question: Why is the Traveller described as 'lonely'?
options: He is the only living human in the supernatural, isolated setting
questions.explanation: He is 'lonely' because he is isolated in the dark forest and receives no response to his human voice.

questions.question: What does the contrast between the loud knocking and the absolute silence highlight?
options: The spooky isolation and barrier between the living and the dead
questions.explanation: The contrast emphasizes the impenetrable barrier between the Traveller (the living) and the phantoms (the supernatural).

questions.question: What action indicates that the house had been abandoned by living beings for a long time?
options: The bird flying out of the turret
questions.explanation: A bird flying out of the turret implies that nature has taken over the building and it has been abandoned by humans.

questions.question: What does the phrase 'voice from the world of men' suggest?
options: There is a world of animals nearby
The listeners belong to a different world, the supernatural world
The house is in a busy city
questions.explanation: It emphasizes that the Traveller belongs to the living world ('world of men'), unlike the phantom listeners.

questions.question: In the 'Warm Up' section, at what time of the day is a hypothetical mysterious scenario described?
questions.explanation: The warm up says, 'You are walking into a grove at 12 noon. You feel that somebody is following you.'

questions.question: How many times does the poem explicitly mention the Traveller knocking or smiting the door?
questions.explanation: He knocks initially ('Knocking on the moonlit door') and then 'smote upon the door again a second time'.

questions.question: What detail shows that the Traveller was looking closely for any sign of life inside?
options: He waited for a head to lean over the leaf-fringed sill
questions.explanation: He expected someone to look down at him from the window ('No head from the leaf-fringed sill Leaned over').

questions.question: HOTS Question: What is the significance of the 'faint moonbeams on the dark stair'?
options: It helps the Traveller see the ghosts
It highlights the eerie emptiness and the shadows where the phantoms gather
It shows that morning is approaching
It proves the house has electricity
questions.explanation: The moonbeams create a spooky lighting effect that emphasizes the dark, empty nature of the house where spirits crowd.

questions.question: HOTS Question: Why does the poet use the word 'host' to describe the listeners?
options: They are welcoming the Traveller as guests
To indicate there is a large, overwhelming number of phantoms present
To show they own the house
To mean they are offering food
questions.explanation: In this context, a 'host' means a large multitude or crowd, intensifying the supernatural presence.

questions.question: HOTS Question: What does the Traveller’s 'perplexed and still' reaction reveal about his state of mind?
options: He knows exactly what to do next
He is terrified and running away
He is confused by the unnatural silence despite sensing a presence
He is simply taking a rest
questions.explanation: He feels an unnatural vibe and is baffled because he expects a human response but gets only eerie silence.

questions.question: What is meant by the 'lone house'?
options: A single, isolated house standing by itself
questions.explanation: 'Lone' means solitary or isolated, emphasizing its remote location in the forest.

questions.question: Which sensory detail does the poet use to break the silence in the first few lines?
options: The sound of the horse champing grasses
The colour of the Traveller's eyes
questions.explanation: The noisy chewing ('champing') of the horse is a sound that contrasts with the deep silence of the forest.

questions.question: What was 'stirred and shaken' by the Traveller's call?
questions.explanation: The poem says, 'Hearkening in an air stirred and shaken / By the lonely Traveller’s call.'

questions.question: How did the Traveller interact with the door?
options: He knocked and smote upon it
questions.explanation: He is described as 'Knocking' and later he 'smote' (knocked loudly) upon it.

questions.question: HOTS Question: The phrase 'dwelt in the lone house then' implies what about the phantoms?
options: They are just visiting for the night
The house is their permanent supernatural residence now
They are hiding from the Traveller
They are trapped in the basement
questions.explanation: The word 'dwelt' means lived or resided, indicating that the spirits are the actual 'inhabitants' of the abandoned house.
